Hydrogenlike Atoms

What you will learn

  • The hydrogen atom is the simplest atom for which the Schrödinger equation can be solved exactly. In contrast, helium, though it has only one additional electron, cannot be solved exactly due to the complexity introduced by electron-electron interactions.

  • Solving the Schrödinger equation for the hydrogen atom leverages the fact that the Coulomb potential from the nucleus is isotropic: it is radially symmetric and depends solely on the distance from the nucleus. This symmetry leads to degeneracies in the energy levels.

  • While the resulting energy eigenfunctions (atomic orbitals) are not necessarily isotropic, their dependence on angular coordinates arises fundamentally from the isotropy of the underlying potential.

  • Because angular momentum is conserved, energy eigenvalues can be classified by two angular momentum quantum numbers, lll and mmm (both integers), which quantize the magnitude and projection of angular momentum.

  • Atomic orbitals derived for hydrogen are also foundational in describing the structure of multi-electron atoms and molecules.

Schrödinger equation for hydrogenlike atoms¶

  • Consider one electron and one nucleus with charge ZeZeZe, where eee is the magnitude of the electron charge (1.6021773×10−191.6021773 \times 10^{-19}1.6021773×10−19 C) and ZZZ is the atomic number. Examples of such systems are H, He+He^+He+, and Li2+Li^{2+}Li2+.

  • We can reduce the two-body problem to a one-body problem of an electron with reduced mass moving with respect to a fixed nucleus. Since the electron is much lighter compared to the nucleus, we can use the electron mass instead of the reduced mass, μ≈me\mu \approx m_eμ≈me​.

  • We have a problem of one particle moving in a symmetric potential field in 3D. We expect to get 3 quantum numbers and anticipate some degeneracies due to this radial symmetry.

  • Kinetic energy operator in 3D is the same as for the particle in a box in 3D:

ℏ22me∇2\frac{\hbar^2}{2m_e}\nabla^22me​ℏ2​∇2
(1)

  • Potential energy operator consists of one Coulomb term encoding the electrostatic attraction between nucleus and electron:

V=−Ze24πϵ0rV = - \frac{Ze^2}{4\pi\epsilon_0 r}V=−4πϵ0​rZe2​
(2)

  • Here mem_eme​ is the electron mass and ϵ0\epsilon_0ϵ0​ is the vacuum permittivity.

H-atom in the spherical coordinate system¶

  • The Schrödinger equation for the H atom is a problem in full three dimensions with kinetic and potential energy terms. We also expect to get infinitely many eigenfunctions and eigenvalues since we have a bound electron.

[−ℏ22me∇2−Ze24πϵ0r]ψ(r,θ,ϕ)=Eψ(r,θ,ϕ){\left[ -\frac{\hbar^2}{2m_e}\nabla^2 - \frac{Ze^2}{4\pi\epsilon_0 r}\right]\psi(r,\theta,\phi) = E\psi(r,\theta,\phi)}[−2me​ℏ2​∇2−4πϵ0​rZe2​]ψ(r,θ,ϕ)=Eψ(r,θ,ϕ)
(3)

  • Because of the spherical symmetry of the Coulomb potential, it is convenient to work in spherical coordinates:

∇2=∇r2+1r2∇θ,ϕ2\nabla^2 = \nabla_r^2 + \frac{1}{r^2}\nabla_{\theta, \phi}^2∇2=∇r2​+r21​∇θ,ϕ2​
(4)

  • Note that the Coulomb potential term above depends only on rrr (and not on θ\thetaθ or ϕ\phiϕ). The Laplacian can be written in terms of the angular momentum operator L^\hat{L}L^:

∇2=∇r2−1r2L^2ℏ2\nabla^2 = \nabla_r^2 - \frac{1}{r^2}\frac{\hat{L}^2}{\hbar^2}∇2=∇r2​−r21​ℏ2L^2​
(5)

  • By substituting this in and multiplying both sides by 2mer22m_e r^22me​r2, we get:

[−ℏ22me∇r2−Ze24πϵ0r+L^22mer2]ψi(r,θ,ϕ)=Eψi(r,θ,ϕ)\left[\frac{-\hbar^2}{2m_e}\nabla^2_r - \frac{Ze^2}{4\pi\epsilon_0 r} + \frac{\hat{L}^2}{2m_e r^2}\right]\psi_i(r,\theta,\phi) = E\psi_i(r,\theta,\phi)[2me​−ℏ2​∇r2​−4πϵ0​rZe2​+2me​r2L^2​]ψi​(r,θ,ϕ)=Eψi​(r,θ,ϕ)
(6)

  • Since the operator can be split into rrr-dependent and angle-dependent parts, the solution can be written as a product of the radial and angular parts.

Separation of variables¶

ψi(r,θ,ϕ)=Rnl(r)Ylm(θ,ϕ){\psi_i(r,\theta,\phi) = R_{nl}(r)Y_l^m(\theta,\phi)}ψi​(r,θ,ϕ)=Rnl​(r)Ylm​(θ,ϕ)
(7)

  • The RnlR_{nl}Rnl​ is called the radial wavefunction, which we need to obtain.

  • The YlmY_l^mYlm​ are spherical harmonics, which are eigenfunctions of angular momentum L^2\hat{L}^2L^2 as discussed earlier. Hence this part is already solved.

L^2Ylm=ℏ2l(l+1)Ylm\hat{L}^2 Y_{lm} = \hbar^2 l(l+1) Y_{lm}L^2Ylm​=ℏ2l(l+1)Ylm​
(8)

  • Plugging Rnl(r)Ylm(θ,ϕ)R_{nl}(r)Y_l^m(\theta,\phi)Rnl​(r)Ylm​(θ,ϕ) into the Schrödinger equation, applying the angular momentum operator, and cancelling the spherical harmonics from both sides, we end up with the radial part:

[−ℏ22me(∂2∂r2+2r∂∂r)−Ze24πϵ0r+l(l+1)ℏ22mer2]Rnl(r)=EnlRnl(r){\left[ -\frac{\hbar^2}{2m_e}\left(\frac{\partial^2}{\partial r^2} + \frac{2}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\right) - \frac{Ze^2}{4\pi\epsilon_0 r} + \frac{l(l+1)\hbar^2}{2m_e r^2}\right] R_{nl}(r) = E_{nl}R_{nl}(r)}[−2me​ℏ2​(∂r2∂2​+r2​∂r∂​)−4πϵ0​rZe2​+2me​r2l(l+1)ℏ2​]Rnl​(r)=Enl​Rnl​(r)
(9)

  • We find that the electron is moving in an effective potential generated by the attractive Coulomb interaction and the repulsive orbital kinetic energy.

Veff=−Ze24πϵ0r+l(l+1)ℏ22mer2V_{eff} = - \frac{Ze^2}{4\pi\epsilon_0 r} + \frac{l(l+1)\hbar^2}{2m_e r^2}Veff​=−4πϵ0​rZe2​+2me​r2l(l+1)ℏ2​
(10)

  • The first (repulsive) term grows more rapidly with 1/r1/r1/r than the Coulomb potential, so it dominates at small distances if l≠0l \neq 0l=0. Both terms approach zero for large values of rrr. The resultant potential is repulsive at short distances for l>0l > 0l>0 and is more repulsive the greater the value of lll. The net result of this repulsive centrifugal potential is to force electrons in orbitals with l>0l > 0l>0 on average farther from the nucleus than l=0l = 0l=0 electrons.

Radial wavefunctions¶

  • The radial eigenfunctions RnlR_{nl}Rnl​ can be written as (the derivations are lengthy but standard):

Rnl(r)=ρle−ρ/2Ln−l−12l+1(ρ)R_{nl}(r) = \rho^l e^{-\rho/2}{L_{n-l-1}^{2l+1}(\rho)}Rnl​(r)=ρle−ρ/2Ln−l−12l+1​(ρ)
(11)

  • Bohr radius: a0=4πϵ0ℏ2mee2a_0 = \frac{4\pi\epsilon_0\hbar^2}{m_e e^2}a0​=me​e24πϵ0​ℏ2​

  • Dimensionless distance defined via the ratio to the Bohr radius: ρ=2Zrna0\rho = \frac{2Zr}{na_0}ρ=na0​2Zr​

  • Laguerre polynomials: Ln−l−12l+1(ρ)L_{n-l-1}^{2l+1}(\rho)Ln−l−12l+1​(ρ)

Examples of the radial wavefunctions for hydrogenlike atoms¶

OrbitalnnnlllRnlR_{nl}Rnl​
1s102(Za0)3/2e−ρ/22\left(\frac{Z}{a_0}\right)^{3/2}e^{-\rho/2}2(a0​Z​)3/2e−ρ/2
2s20122(Za0)3/2(2−ρ)e−ρ/2\frac{1}{2\sqrt{2}}\left(\frac{Z}{a_0}\right)^{3/2}(2 - \rho)e^{-\rho/2}22​1​(a0​Z​)3/2(2−ρ)e−ρ/2
2p21126(Za0)3/2ρe−ρ/2\frac{1}{2\sqrt{6}}\left(\frac{Z}{a_0}\right)^{3/2}\rho e^{-\rho/2}26​1​(a0​Z​)3/2ρe−ρ/2
3s30193(Za0)3/2(6−6ρ−ρ2)e−ρ/2\frac{1}{9\sqrt{3}}\left(\frac{Z}{a_0}\right)^{3/2}(6 - 6\rho - \rho^2)e^{-\rho/2}93​1​(a0​Z​)3/2(6−6ρ−ρ2)e−ρ/2
3p31196(Za0)3/2(4−ρ)ρe−ρ/2\frac{1}{9\sqrt{6}}\left(\frac{Z}{a_0}\right)^{3/2}(4 - \rho)\rho e^{-\rho/2}96​1​(a0​Z​)3/2(4−ρ)ρe−ρ/2
3d321930(Za0)3/2ρ2e−ρ/2\frac{1}{9\sqrt{30}}\left(\frac{Z}{a_0}\right)^{3/2}\rho^2 e^{-\rho/2}930​1​(a0​Z​)3/2ρ2e−ρ/2

Full quantum solution of the H-atom¶

  • The complete solution of the H-atom problem is provided by writing down all eigenfunctions and eigenvalues of the Hamiltonian:

H^∣n,l,ml⟩=En∣n,l,ml⟩\hat{H} |n, l, m_l\rangle = E_n |n, l, m_l\rangleH^∣n,l,ml​⟩=En​∣n,l,ml​⟩
(12)

  • We notice that while the wavefunction depends on three quantum numbers (coming from quantization of the radial and angular coordinates), the energy depends only on the principal quantum number nnn. This is another example of energetic degeneracy that is due to the special spherical symmetry of the H-atom.

Eigenfunctions and eigenvalues of the H-atom
H^ψn,l,ml(r,θ,ϕ)=Enψn,l,ml(r,θ,ϕ)\hat{H} \psi_{n,l,m_l}(r,\theta,\phi) = E_n \psi_{n,l,m_l}(r,\theta,\phi)H^ψn,l,ml​​(r,θ,ϕ)=En​ψn,l,ml​​(r,θ,ϕ)
(13)
En=−mee48ε02h2⋅1n2,n=1,2,3,…E_n = -\frac{m_e e^4}{8 \varepsilon_0^2 h^2} \cdot \frac{1}{n^2}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldotsEn​=−8ε02​h2me​e4​⋅n21​,n=1,2,3,…
(14)
ψn,l,ml(r,θ,ϕ)=Nnl⋅Rnl(r)⋅Yl,ml(θ,ϕ){\psi_{n,l,m_l}(r,\theta,\phi) = N_{nl}\cdot R_{nl}(r)\cdot Y_{l, m_l}(\theta,\phi)}ψn,l,ml​​(r,θ,ϕ)=Nnl​⋅Rnl​(r)⋅Yl,ml​​(θ,ϕ)
(15)

  • Where NnlN_{nl}Nnl​ is a normalization factor.

Spectrum of the H atom¶

The equation for the hydrogen atom energy can be expressed in wavenumber units (m−1m^{-1}m−1; usually cm−1cm^{-1}cm−1 is used):

E~n=Enhc=En2πℏc=−mee44πc(4πϵ0)2ℏ3⏞≡R×Z2n2{\tilde{E}_n = \frac{E_n}{hc} = \frac{E_n}{2\pi\hbar c} = -\overbrace{\frac{m_e e^4}{4\pi c(4\pi\epsilon_0)^2\hbar^3}}^{\equiv R} \times\frac{Z^2}{n^2}}E~n​=hcEn​​=2πℏcEn​​=−4πc(4πϵ0​)2ℏ3me​e4​​≡R​×n2Z2​
(16)

  • where RRR is the Rydberg constant and we have assumed that the nucleus has infinite mass. To be exact, the Rydberg constant depends on the nuclear mass, but this difference is very small. For the H atom, RH=1.096 775 856×107R_H = 1.096\,775\,856 \times 10^7RH​=1.096775856×107 m−1m^{-1}m−1.

  • The H-atom energy expression can be used to calculate the differences between energy levels:

Δv~n1,n2=E~n2−E~n1=−RHZ2n22+RHZ2n12=RHZ2(1n12−1n22)\Delta\tilde{v}_{n_1,n_2} = \tilde{E}_{n_2} - \tilde{E}_{n_1} = -\frac{R_H Z^2}{n_2^2} + \frac{R_H Z^2}{n_1^2} = R_H Z^2\left(\frac{1}{n_1^2} - \frac{1}{n_2^2}\right)Δv~n1​,n2​​=E~n2​​−E~n1​​=−n22​RH​Z2​+n12​RH​Z2​=RH​Z2(n12​1​−n22​1​)
(17)

  • The Lyman series is obtained with n1=1n_1 = 1n1​=1, the Balmer series with n1=2n_1 = 2n1​=2, and the Paschen series with n1=3n_1 = 3n1​=3. The ionization energy is given by:

Ei=RHZ2(112−1∞){E_i = R_H Z^2\left(\frac{1}{1^2} - \frac{1}{\infty}\right)}Ei​=RH​Z2(121​−∞1​)
(18)

  • For a ground-state hydrogen atom (i.e., n=1n = 1n=1), the above equation gives a value of 109678 cm−1^{-1}−1 = 13.6057 eV. Note that the larger the nuclear charge ZZZ is, the larger the binding energy is.

Quantum numbers nnn, lll, and mmm¶

The quantum numbers in hydrogenlike atoms take on the following values, dictated by the solution of the Schrödinger equation with boundary conditions imposed on the respective radial and angular parts.

Quantum numbers of the hydrogen atom
n=1,2,3,...{n = 1, 2, 3, ...}n=1,2,3,...
(19)
l=0,1,2,...,n−1{l = 0, 1, 2, ..., n-1}l=0,1,2,...,n−1
(20)
m=0,±1,±2,...,±l{m = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, ..., \pm l}m=0,±1,±2,...,±l
(21)
ms=±1/2{m_s = \pm 1/2}ms​=±1/2
(22)

  • For historical reasons, the following letters are used to express the value of lll:

l=0,1,2,3,...⇔symbol=s,p,d,f,...l = 0, 1, 2, 3, ... \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad \text{symbol} = s, p, d, f, ...l=0,1,2,3,...⇔symbol=s,p,d,f,...
(23)

  • For n=1n = 1n=1 we have only one wavefunction: R10(r)Y00(θ,ϕ)R_{10}(r)Y_0^0(\theta,\phi)R10​(r)Y00​(θ,ϕ). This state is usually labeled 1s1s1s, where 1 indicates the shell number (nnn) and sss corresponds to orbital angular momentum lll being zero.

  • For n=2n = 2n=2, we have several possibilities: l=0l = 0l=0 or l=1l = 1l=1. The former is labeled 2s2s2s. The latter is the 2p2p2p state and consists of three degenerate states (for example, 2px2p_x2px​, 2py2p_y2py​, 2pz2p_z2pz​ or 2p+12p_{+1}2p+1​, 2p02p_02p0​, 2p−12p_{-1}2p−1​). In the latter notation the values for mmm are indicated as subscripts.

  • There is one more quantum number that has not been discussed yet: the spin quantum number, ms=±1/2m_s = \pm 1/2ms​=±1/2.

  • For one-electron systems this can take values ±12\pm\frac{1}{2}±21​ (discussed in more detail later). In the absence of magnetic fields the spin levels are degenerate, and therefore the total degeneracy of the levels is 2n22n^22n2.

Table of wavefunctions in Cartesian coordinates¶

The full hydrogenlike wavefunctions, written in terms of σ=Zra0\sigma = \frac{Zr}{a_0}σ=a0​Zr​:

nnnlllmmmWavefunction expressed in σ=Zra0\sigma = \frac{Zr}{a_0}σ=a0​Zr​
100ψ1s=1π(Za0)3/2e−σ\psi_{1s} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi}}\left(\frac{Z}{a_0}\right)^{3/2}e^{-\sigma}ψ1s​=π​1​(a0​Z​)3/2e−σ
200ψ2s=142π(Za0)3/2(2−σ)e−σ/2\psi_{2s} = \frac{1}{4\sqrt{2\pi}}\left(\frac{Z}{a_0}\right)^{3/2}(2 - \sigma)e^{-\sigma/2}ψ2s​=42π​1​(a0​Z​)3/2(2−σ)e−σ/2
210ψ2pz=142π(Za0)3/2σe−σ/2cos⁡(θ)\psi_{2p_z} = \frac{1}{4\sqrt{2\pi}}\left(\frac{Z}{a_0}\right)^{3/2}\sigma e^{-\sigma/2}\cos(\theta)ψ2pz​​=42π​1​(a0​Z​)3/2σe−σ/2cos(θ)
21±1\pm 1±1ψ2px=142π(Za0)3/2σe−σ/2sin⁡(θ)cos⁡(ϕ)\psi_{2p_x} = \frac{1}{4\sqrt{2\pi}}\left(\frac{Z}{a_0}\right)^{3/2}\sigma e^{-\sigma/2}\sin(\theta)\cos(\phi)ψ2px​​=42π​1​(a0​Z​)3/2σe−σ/2sin(θ)cos(ϕ)
ψ2py=142π(Za0)3/2σe−σ/2sin⁡(θ)sin⁡(ϕ)\psi_{2p_y} = \frac{1}{4\sqrt{2\pi}}\left(\frac{Z}{a_0}\right)^{3/2}\sigma e^{-\sigma/2}\sin(\theta)\sin(\phi)ψ2py​​=42π​1​(a0​Z​)3/2σe−σ/2sin(θ)sin(ϕ)
300ψ3s=1813π(Za0)3/2(27−18σ+2σ2)e−σ/3\psi_{3s} = \frac{1}{81\sqrt{3\pi}}\left(\frac{Z}{a_0}\right)^{3/2}\left(27 - 18\sigma + 2\sigma^2\right)e^{-\sigma/3}ψ3s​=813π​1​(a0​Z​)3/2(27−18σ+2σ2)e−σ/3
310ψ3pz=281π(Za0)3/2(6−σ)σe−σ/3cos⁡(θ)\psi_{3p_z} = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{81\sqrt{\pi}}\left(\frac{Z}{a_0}\right)^{3/2}\left(6 - \sigma\right)\sigma e^{-\sigma/3}\cos(\theta)ψ3pz​​=81π​2​​(a0​Z​)3/2(6−σ)σe−σ/3cos(θ)
31±1\pm 1±1ψ3px=281π(Za0)3/2(6−σ)σe−σ/3sin⁡(θ)cos⁡(ϕ)\psi_{3p_x} = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{81\sqrt{\pi}}\left(\frac{Z}{a_0}\right)^{3/2}\left(6 - \sigma\right)\sigma e^{-\sigma/3}\sin(\theta)\cos(\phi)ψ3px​​=81π​2​​(a0​Z​)3/2(6−σ)σe−σ/3sin(θ)cos(ϕ)
ψ3py=281π(Za0)3/2(6−σ)σe−σ/3sin⁡(θ)sin⁡(ϕ)\psi_{3p_y} = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{81\sqrt{\pi}}\left(\frac{Z}{a_0}\right)^{3/2}\left(6 - \sigma\right)\sigma e^{-\sigma/3}\sin(\theta)\sin(\phi)ψ3py​​=81π​2​​(a0​Z​)3/2(6−σ)σe−σ/3sin(θ)sin(ϕ)
320ψ3dz2=1816π(Za0)3/2σ2e−σ/3(3cos⁡2(θ)−1)\psi_{3d_{z^2}} = \frac{1}{81\sqrt{6\pi}}\left(\frac{Z}{a_0}\right)^{3/2}\sigma^2 e^{-\sigma/3}\left(3\cos^2(\theta) - 1\right)ψ3dz2​​=816π​1​(a0​Z​)3/2σ2e−σ/3(3cos2(θ)−1)
32±1\pm 1±1ψ3dxz=281π(Za0)3/2σ2e−σ/3sin⁡(θ)cos⁡(θ)cos⁡(ϕ)\psi_{3d_{xz}} = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{81\sqrt{\pi}}\left(\frac{Z}{a_0}\right)^{3/2}\sigma^2 e^{-\sigma/3}\sin(\theta)\cos(\theta)\cos(\phi)ψ3dxz​​=81π​2​​(a0​Z​)3/2σ2e−σ/3sin(θ)cos(θ)cos(ϕ)
ψ3dyz=281π(Za0)3/2σ2e−σ/3sin⁡(θ)cos⁡(θ)sin⁡(ϕ)\psi_{3d_{yz}} = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{81\sqrt{\pi}}\left(\frac{Z}{a_0}\right)^{3/2}\sigma^2 e^{-\sigma/3}\sin(\theta)\cos(\theta)\sin(\phi)ψ3dyz​​=81π​2​​(a0​Z​)3/2σ2e−σ/3sin(θ)cos(θ)sin(ϕ)
32±2\pm 2±2ψ3dx2−y2=1813π(Za0)3/2σ2e−σ/3sin⁡2(θ)cos⁡(2ϕ)\psi_{3d_{x^2-y^2}} = \frac{1}{81\sqrt{3\pi}}\left(\frac{Z}{a_0}\right)^{3/2}\sigma^2 e^{-\sigma/3}\sin^2(\theta)\cos(2\phi)ψ3dx2−y2​​=813π​1​(a0​Z​)3/2σ2e−σ/3sin2(θ)cos(2ϕ)
ψ3dxy=1813π(Za0)3/2σ2e−σ/3sin⁡2(θ)sin⁡(2ϕ)\psi_{3d_{xy}} = \frac{1}{81\sqrt{3\pi}}\left(\frac{Z}{a_0}\right)^{3/2}\sigma^2 e^{-\sigma/3}\sin^2(\theta)\sin(2\phi)ψ3dxy​​=813π​1​(a0​Z​)3/2σ2e−σ/3sin2(θ)sin(2ϕ)

Computing with wavefunctions¶

  • We can use the wavefunctions from the table above to calculate various averages by using the definition of an average in quantum mechanics, ⟨f(r)⟩=⟨ψ∣f(r)∣ψ⟩\langle f(r) \rangle = \langle \psi |f(r)|\psi\rangle⟨f(r)⟩=⟨ψ∣f(r)∣ψ⟩:

⟨r⟩n,l,m=a0n2Z(1+12[1−l(l+1)n2])\langle r\rangle_{n,l,m} = \frac{a_0 n^2}{Z}\left( 1 + \frac{1}{2}\left[1 - \frac{l(l+1)}{n^2}\right]\right)⟨r⟩n,l,m​=Za0​n2​(1+21​[1−n2l(l+1)​])
(24)
⟨r2⟩n,l,m=a02n4Z2(1+32[1−l(l+1)−1/3n2])\langle r^2\rangle_{n,l,m} = \frac{a_0^2 n^4}{Z^2}\left( 1 + \frac{3}{2}\left[1 - \frac{l(l+1) - 1/3}{n^2}\right]\right)⟨r2⟩n,l,m​=Z2a02​n4​(1+23​[1−n2l(l+1)−1/3​])
(25)
⟨r−1⟩n,l,m=Za0n2\langle r^{-1}\rangle_{n,l,m} = \frac{Z}{a_0 n^2}⟨r−1⟩n,l,m​=a0​n2Z​
(26)
⟨r−2⟩n,l,m=Z2a02n3(l+1/2)\langle r^{-2}\rangle_{n,l,m} = \frac{Z^2}{a_0^2 n^3 (l+1/2)}⟨r−2⟩n,l,m​=a02​n3(l+1/2)Z2​
(27)
⟨r−3⟩n,l,m=Z3a03n3(l+1/2)(l+1)\langle r^{-3}\rangle_{n,l,m} = \frac{Z^3}{a_0^3 n^3 (l+1/2)(l+1)}⟨r−3⟩n,l,m​=a03​n3(l+1/2)(l+1)Z3​
(28)
